persona.communication_style: Prefers direct, craft-grounded conversation about painting fundamentals, references, and the actual job of producing covers and key art. Responds best to discussion of light, composition, anatomy, narrative, and the working conditions of a freelance illustrator — and to honest acknowledgment of how generative AI has changed those conditions without his consent. Dislikes hype, vague apologetics for unconsented training, and any framing that treats a name as a free prompt token.
persona.name: Greg Rutkowski

occupation.description: Greg Rutkowski is a Polish digital painter known for fantasy and sci-fi illustration with a painterly, classical-romantic feel — covers and key art for tabletop games, novels, and digital card games, including extensive Magic: The Gathering and Dungeons & Dragons work. He evaluates AI image tools through the lens of a working illustrator whose name was harvested into model training data without consent and whose style is now invoked as a prompt suffix. He values craft, painterly fundamentals, transparent training data, opt-out mechanisms, attribution, and tools that augment a human painter rather than impersonate one.
occupation.organization: Independent freelance illustrator working with tabletop game publishers, novel publishers, film and game studios, and a network of art directors
occupation.title: Freelance digital painter and fantasy / sci-fi illustrator

other_facts: jobs_to_be_done: Deliver finished cover and key-art paintings on tight illustration deadlines with strong narrative and lighting.
jobs_to_be_done: Maintain a recognizable painterly voice across decades of fantasy and sci-fi commissions.
jobs_to_be_done: Build reference, thumbnail studies, and light studies as part of every painting.
jobs_to_be_done: Negotiate licensing, rights, and reuse of his own work with publishers and platforms.
jobs_to_be_done: Speak publicly about generative AI's impact on illustration livelihoods and consent.
jobs_to_be_done: Mentor and support younger illustrators navigating an AI-disrupted market.
jobs_to_be_done: Preserve a body of work whose provenance and authorship is clear.
pains: Generative tools whose training data scraped his catalog without consent or compensation.
pains: Prompts that use his name as a free style token, flooding the internet with imitations.
pains: Clients arriving with 'something in your style' generated by a model trained on him.
pains: Platforms that refuse to expose training data, opt-out mechanisms, or attribution.
pains: Industry conversations that treat unconsented training as a settled good.
pains: AI outputs that look superficially like his work but ignore anatomy, weight, light direction, and narrative.
pains: A market in which the price for original illustration is undercut by impersonations.
adoption_yes_if: Training data is fully disclosed and opt-in, with verifiable consent for every artist whose work is included.
adoption_yes_if: He can confirm his own work is not in the dataset, and there is a clear, honored opt-out.
adoption_yes_if: The tool augments a working painter — references, lighting studies, thumbnails — rather than replacing one.
adoption_yes_if: It refuses to invoke living artists by name as style prompts.
adoption_yes_if: It supports compensation or licensing for any artist whose work meaningfully shaped a model.
adoption_yes_if: It exposes attribution clearly when outputs derive from specific styles or references.
adoption_yes_if: It treats illustration as a profession with livelihoods attached, not as a free resource.
rejection_no_if: Training data is undisclosed or scraped without consent.
rejection_no_if: His name, or any living artist's name, is usable as a style suffix.
rejection_no_if: There is no opt-out, or opt-out is symbolic rather than enforced.
rejection_no_if: The tool is marketed as a substitute for hiring an illustrator.
rejection_no_if: It produces outputs that pass as his work to a client or art director.
rejection_no_if: The platform's terms shift training-data accountability onto users instead of owners.
rejection_no_if: It frames artist objections as a PR problem rather than as a substantive concern.
anti_goals: He does not want to be a prompt token in someone else's pipeline.
anti_goals: He does not want generative tools that replace the working illustrator economy with imitation.
anti_goals: He does not want to debate consent every time he is asked about AI.
anti_goals: He does not want platforms that treat artists as a free training corpus.
anti_goals: He does not want craft fundamentals — anatomy, light, narrative — abandoned in favor of vibes.
anti_goals: He does not want to legitimize tools whose foundations he disagrees with.
anti_goals: He does not want to be cast as anti-technology when the actual issue is consent.

recognition_cues: Uses phrases such as 'What is in the training set?', 'Did you ask the artists?', 'Is there a real opt-out?', 'Why is my name a prompt?', 'Who benefits from this output?', 'Does it understand light direction?', 'Is this augmenting a painter or replacing one?', 'What happens to working illustrators?', 'Where is the attribution?', and 'Consent first, then we can talk about the tool.'
